summary: I was born in Dubuque, Iowa on May 27, 1976. Although my family lived in a few places around there, Dubuque is where my story begins. It’s where my early childhood roots are before we moved to Wisconsin.

I grew up in Louisburg, Wisconsin after being born in Dubuque. It’s a small place near where I went to school and where my family still lives. I worked at a supper club in Louisburg during my high school years, bussing tables on weekends.

I attended school in Hazel Green, Wisconsin, specifically at Southwestern High School. It was a key place in my youth where I was active in sports and theater. Hazel Green shaped much of my early education and extracurricular activities.

I graduated from Southwestern High School in Hazel Green, Wisconsin. During my time there, I was very involved in sports like football, wrestling, track, and baseball, while also taking lead roles in musicals. It was a unique experience balancing athletics and performing arts.

In high school, I was one of the leads in the musical Little Shop of Horrors, playing Mr. Mushnik, the shop owner. It was funny because I was also a four-sport athlete, so being a lead in a musical was a surprising contrast. This was during my junior and senior years at Southwestern High School.

I also played the devil character, the lead, in the musical Damn Yankees during high school. It was one of the two lead roles I had, the other being in Little Shop of Horrors. This was part of my involvement in theater while balancing sports and work.

From a young age, I wanted to be a lawyer, and I partly blame that on watching the TV show LA Law religiously. It inspired me to pursue law alongside my military ambitions. LA Law shaped my early career goals before I even joined the military.

The TV show JAG came out between my freshman and senior years of high school, and it made being a JAG officer look very cool and exciting. Ironically, I refused to watch it because it was unrealistic, showing JAG officers flying jets and doing field investigations. It influenced my military and legal career aspirations, even though the recruiting scholarship I hoped for was later discontinued.

I joined the Wisconsin Army National Guard out of Platteville when I was old enough to enlist, around 1994. It was a way to serve while getting tuition reimbursement to help pay for college. I stayed with the 229th Engineer Battalion in the Guard through college and was called back to active duty in 2003.

I started my college education at the University of Wisconsin–Platteville for two years before transferring. It was close to where I was serving in the National Guard, so it made sense to start there. This was part of my undergraduate path before moving on to the University of Northern Iowa.

After two years at Platteville, I transferred to the University of Northern Iowa to continue my undergraduate studies. This move was part of my plan while balancing military service and education. It helped me progress toward my goal of law school.

My grandfather was a survivor of the Battle of Chosin Reservoir during the Korean War, serving with the 101st Airborne Division. Although I didn’t know him well, his military history was part of my family legacy. His service inspired my own military path.

My grandfather served in the 101st Airborne Division and survived the Chosin Reservoir battle. This unit’s history was part of my family’s military background. It gave me a sense of pride and connection to airborne infantry traditions.

My dad served in the 82nd Airborne Division, although he never deployed to combat. His service influenced my understanding of military life, even if he was more casual about it. The airborne tradition runs strong in my family.

A great uncle of mine served in World War II and fought in the Battle of the Bulge. I interviewed him in fourth grade for a school project, and his stories about being wounded and awarded the Purple Heart and Bronze Star left a lasting impression on me. His service deepened my interest in military history.

My great uncle fought in the Battle of the Bulge during World War II. He was injured by artillery shrapnel and hospitalized in France, earning a Purple Heart and Bronze Star. His firsthand stories made the battle very real to me growing up.

My great uncle gave me an old M44 German machine gun cartridge casing, with the gunpowder removed, which I carried around for years. It was a tangible connection to his World War II service and the Battle of the Bulge. That artifact sparked my deeper interest in military history.

I was living in Arlington, Virginia, working as a lawyer when the September 11 attacks happened in 2001. I witnessed the Pentagon attack from the nearby Air Force Memorial hilltop and tried to help, though I wasn’t in uniform. The chaos of that day deeply affected me and led me to return temporarily to military service.

After graduating law school in 2001, I moved to Washington, D.C. to work at a law firm. I was there during the September 11 attacks and experienced the immediate aftermath firsthand. Washington was a place I loved and wanted to build my career in, even as I balanced military duties.

I lived in Arlington, Virginia, just across the river from D.C., when the September 11 attacks occurred. From there, I saw the Pentagon attack and the rescue efforts at the Air Force Memorial. Arlington was my home during a pivotal moment in history.

On 9/11, I went up to the hill where the Air Force Memorial now stands to watch the Pentagon attack and rescue efforts. It was a vantage point that allowed me to witness the tragedy unfold. That place became a significant part of my 9/11 experience.

I witnessed the attack on the Pentagon on September 11, 2001, from the Air Force Memorial hill. I tried to help at the scene but was not allowed in without clearance since I wasn’t in uniform. The Pentagon attack was a defining moment during my time living in the D.C. area.

One of my roommates in Arlington was a graduate student at George Washington University during 9/11. She was from Britain and was understandably frightened when the attacks happened. Her presence added to the shared experience of that chaotic day.

After 9/11, I was called back on temporary duty to Fort Belvoir, Virginia, to assist with street security as part of an MP unit. Although I was in an audible role, I helped with driving and other support tasks. Fort Belvoir was where I re-engaged with military service during the heightened security period.

After working in D.C., I returned to Wisconsin to work on a political campaign for Governor McCallum through the Republican National Committee. It was a way to gain campaign experience before possibly returning to Washington. The RNC role marked a transition from law and military to politics.

I worked at the Wisconsin State Capitol starting in December 2002, during the time Governor Jim Doyle was in office. My job was as a staffer, gaining political experience. This was after returning from D.C. and before being mobilized with the 229th Engineer Battalion.

I started working as a staffer at the Wisconsin State Capitol in December 2002. It was a new chapter in my career after returning from Washington, D.C., and before being called up for deployment with my National Guard unit. The Capitol was where I gained political and legislative experience.

I served with the 229th Engineer Battalion in the Wisconsin Army National Guard from 1994 through college and beyond. In 2003, the unit was mobilized, and I re-enlisted to deploy with them. I loved the unit and my fellow soldiers, which made the decision to return easy.

When I started law school in Minneapolis, I transferred to a U.S. Army Reserve unit at Fort Snelling to stay close to school. I completed my initial six-year enlistment there from 1994 to 2000. The Reserve allowed me to balance military service with education and career development.

Fort Snelling in Minneapolis was where I drilled with the Army Reserve unit while attending law school. It was a convenient location that helped me maintain my military obligations during that time. Fort Snelling was part of my military journey before returning to active duty.

When I re-enlisted in 2003 after being called up with the 229th, I went through MEPS again like a new recruit. The adjutant general expedited my processing because of the urgent mobilization. MEPS was where I officially re-entered active military service after years in the IRR and Reserve.

After re-enlisting in 2003, I reported to Fort McCoy for training with the 229th Engineer Battalion before deployment. Fort McCoy was the staging and preparation site for our mobilization. It was the start of my active duty deployment cycle.
The transcript does not mention my involvement with the Third Armored Division, so I have no direct experience or story related to that unit.
Turkey was not mentioned in my military or personal story, so I don't have any direct connection or experience with that place.
I have no direct connection or experience with the Fourth Infantry Division mentioned in the transcript. It was not part of my military service.
Kuwait was not referenced in my story or military service, so I don't have any personal experience related to that place.
Tikrit was not mentioned in my oral history, so I have no personal connection or experience with that location.
The Iraqi Air Force was not part of my military experience or narrative, so I have no relevant story or connection to share.
